1. Introduction

The College Event Aggregator is a role-based web application designed to centralize event planning, publication, participation, feedback collection, notifications, and reporting for a college or multi-department academic institution.

1.1 Company Profile / Institute Profile / Client Profile

The intended client for this system is a higher education institution that conducts frequent technical, cultural, sports, seminar, workshop, conference, and competition events across multiple departments. In such an environment, administrative oversight and department-level autonomy must coexist. The college administration needs visibility into user approval, system-wide statistics, and audit-ready reports; faculty coordinators require operational control over the events they own; and students need a single place to discover opportunities, register, complete payment when fees apply, and submit structured feedback after the event.

The repository confirms that the system is tailored to an academic client rather than a generic public ticketing platform. Role names, dashboard behaviour, department filters, course and year fields, faculty approval state, and feedback/report workflows all indicate a campus-focused client profile.

1.2 Abstract

The College Event Aggregator addresses the operational fragmentation that commonly occurs in academic event management. The current implementation combines a React frontend, an Express backend, and a MySQL database to provide a unified platform for event creation, user approval, student registration, media uploads, notifications, dynamic feedback forms, and PDF report generation. Admin users control faculty onboarding and system oversight, faculty users create and manage events for their departments, and student users browse upcoming activities, register once per event, view notification updates, and submit feedback after completion. The backend schema includes dedicated tables for users, events, gallery images, participations, legacy feedback, dynamic feedback forms, questions, responses, answers, and notifications. Together, these modules deliver an operationally meaningful academic event management information system that improves transparency, reduces duplicate work, and produces reusable documentation for institutional review.

1.3 Existing System and Need for System

Existing System Characteristics

  • Events are often announced through notice boards, messaging groups, and department-specific spreadsheets.
  • Faculty approvals and student participation are tracked separately, which causes duplication and inconsistent records.
  • Payment collection for paid events is rarely linked back to the event record in a structured manner.
  • Feedback is usually collected through ad hoc forms that are disconnected from registration data and event ownership.
  • Preparing post-event reports requires manual data compilation from multiple sources.

Need for the Proposed System

  • A single platform is required to unify user approval, event creation, registration, and feedback.
  • Department coordinators need controlled autonomy without losing administrative accountability.
  • Students need reliable registration status, searchable event discovery, and post-event engagement history.
  • Institutions require traceable data for evaluation, accreditation records, and management reporting.
  • The event lifecycle should end with actionable insights rather than scattered spreadsheets.

1.4 Scope of System

  • Support admin, faculty, and student roles with distinct dashboards and permissions.
  • Cover the full event lifecycle from publication to report generation.
  • Store event images, payment QR codes, participation records, feedback forms, and report files.
  • Enable filtering by category, department, and time window to improve discoverability.
  • Generate downloadable event reports for institutional review and archival needs.

The scope is intentionally operational rather than promotional. The system is built to serve college administration and faculty coordination workflows, not public marketing campaigns or external ticket marketplaces. It is therefore well suited to departmental festivals, technical competitions, seminars, and student engagement drives inside an academic environment.

1.5 Operating Environment - Hardware and Software

Deployment LayerRecommended HardwareSoftware / Runtime
Client deviceIntel i3 / Ryzen 3 or above, 4 GB RAM, 1366 x 768 displayChrome, Edge, or Firefox with JavaScript enabled
Faculty and admin workstationIntel i5 / Ryzen 5 or above, 8 GB RAM, SSD storageWindows 10/11 or Ubuntu 22.04+, browser, PDF reader
Application server2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM, 50 GB SSDNode.js runtime, Express application, static uploads storage
Database server2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM, backup-enabled storageMySQL 8.x Community Server
NetworkBroadband or campus LANSMTP access for Gmail-based notifications

1.6 Brief Description of Technology Used

The repository shows a modern three-tier implementation: React for the presentation layer, Express for the application/service layer, and MySQL for persistence, with Multer, Nodemailer, and PDFKit extending the system for uploads, mail, and report generation.

1.6.1 Operating Systems Used

The codebase is portable across Windows and Unix-like environments because the frontend is a browser application and the backend runs on Node.js. The current workspace is Windows-based, while a production deployment can be hosted on Ubuntu or another Linux server with the same Node and MySQL runtime stack.

1.6.2 DBMS / NoSQL Used to Build Database

The project uses MySQL as the sole persistence engine. The design is relational because user roles, events, registrations, feedback, and notifications all require referential integrity, indexed joins, and reliable transactional behaviour. No NoSQL store is used in the present repository.

TechnologyUsage in the System
React 19.2.0Frontend component rendering and dashboard/page composition
React Router DOM 7.10.1Role-based routing and protected page navigation
Axios 1.13.2HTTP client for backend REST integration
Tailwind CSS 4.1.17Utility-first styling for UI consistency
Vite 7.2.4Frontend build tool and development server
Node.jsJavaScript runtime for the backend
Express 4.22.1REST API framework and middleware orchestration
MySQL2 3.6.0Database connector and promise-based query execution
MySQLRelational data storage for events, users, registrations, feedback, and notifications
Multer 1.4.5-lts.1Handling event images, payment QR, and documents
Nodemailer 7.0.12Event alert and registration email dispatch
PDFKit 0.14.0Server-side event report generation in PDF format
bcryptjs 2.4.3Password hashing for non-admin user accounts

2. Proposed System

The proposed system is a centralized, web-based academic event management information system that connects administrative control, department-level event execution, student participation, and report-ready data capture.

2.1 Study of Similar Systems

Similar systems range from manual spreadsheet processes to public event booking platforms. However, each alternative leaves a gap when applied to department-level academic operations. The study below compares the project against the most common reference approaches.

Reference SystemStrengthsObserved LimitationHow This Project Responds
Manual notice board + spreadsheetsLow technical barrier and familiar to departmentsNo centralized approvals, no duplicate control, no live status or reportsThe project automates approvals, validations, and consolidated reporting
Google Forms with shared sheetsQuick collection of registrations and feedbackDifferent forms for each activity, disconnected from events and users, difficult to auditThe project keeps event, participant, and feedback records in one relational model
Generic event booking platformsStrong registration flow and ticketingNot tailored for college departments, faculty approvals, or academic workflowsThe project supports institute-specific roles, departments, and academic categories
Campus ERP notice modulesIntegrated with institutional usersOften limited to announcements without payment QR, custom feedback, or rich reportsThe project adds event media, feedback forms, and PDF analytics

2.2 Feasibility Study

Feasibility TypeAssessmentProject-Specific Observation
TechnicalHighThe chosen stack already exists in the repository and uses mature libraries such as React, Express, MySQL, Multer, Nodemailer, and PDFKit. The schema supports the full workflow including feedback and notifications.
EconomicHighThe system is built predominantly with open-source software. Licensing cost is near zero, and recurring cost is limited to hosting, storage, backup, and institutional email configuration.
OperationalHighThe user roles map directly to college operations: admin governs faculty access, faculty manage events, and students browse, register, and respond. The UI already reflects these operational boundaries.

2.3 Objectives of Proposed System

  • Create a single source of truth for college events and event-related transactions.
  • Provide a clear approval workflow for faculty coordinators before they access operational features.
  • Enable fast event publication with category, department, schedule, media, and fee metadata.
  • Reduce duplicate registrations and feedback entries through controlled checks.
  • Support event-specific dynamic feedback forms instead of a one-size-fits-all survey.
  • Generate structured PDF reports for management review and archival needs.
  • Improve student visibility into upcoming, past, and completed events.
  • Capture notification and participation history in a reusable, queryable model.

2.4 Users of System

User TypeMain Responsibilities
AdminApprove faculty, manage users, view system statistics, create or delete events, generate reports
FacultyCreate and edit events, upload gallery images and payment QR, define feedback forms, review event output
StudentRegister independently or as a team member, receive notifications, submit event feedback, view registrations and history

3. Analysis and Design

This chapter translates repository behaviour into formal requirements, database design, and structural diagrams.

3.1 Functional Requirements

IDRequirement
FR-01The system shall allow student and faculty sign-up with server-side validation and role-specific fields.
FR-02The system shall require admin approval before a faculty account can log in.
FR-03The system shall allow admin and faculty users to create, edit, publish, and delete event records.
FR-04The system shall allow event creators to upload up to ten gallery images and one payment QR image.
FR-05The system shall support event categorization, department tagging, date-based filtering, and search.
FR-06The system shall allow students to register for an event once and prevent duplicate registrations.
FR-07The system shall send student notifications and email alerts when a new event is published.
FR-08The system shall allow faculty or admin users to define dynamic feedback forms per event.
FR-09The system shall allow students to submit one feedback response per event and preserve answer-level detail.
FR-10The system shall allow admin users to review event participants and user accounts through dedicated dashboards.
FR-11The system shall generate downloadable PDF reports that summarize event details, participants, gallery images, and feedback.
FR-12The system shall maintain separate dashboards for admin, faculty, and student roles.

3.1 Non-Functional Requirements

CategoryRequirement Detail
NFR-01 SecurityInput validation, password hashing for non-admin users, role-based routing, restricted faculty approval workflow
NFR-02 PerformanceConnection pooling, indexed tables, and asynchronous notification/email dispatch keep response paths practical for campus-scale use
NFR-03 AvailabilityHealth endpoint, static asset serving, and modular routes support reliable development and deployment
NFR-04 UsabilityRole-specific dashboards, clear action buttons, filters, and card/table layout reduce user training effort
NFR-05 MaintainabilityFrontend pages and backend controllers are grouped by responsibility; schema is clearly separated in main_Schema.txt
NFR-06 ScalabilityMySQL schema supports many events and registrations; file storage is separated from relational metadata
NFR-07 PortabilityThe stack runs on Windows or Linux with Node.js and MySQL, while the frontend is browser-based
NFR-08 AuditabilityRegistrations, feedback submissions, notifications, and report generation are stored with timestamps

3.2 Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD)

The database revolves around events as the primary operational entity, while users anchor authentication, ownership, participation, and notifications. A legacy feedback table exists alongside the newer dynamic feedback subsystem, allowing the product to evolve without discarding earlier event records.

3.3 Database Design and Table Structures

The schema is normalized into identity, event, transaction, evaluation, and notification subdomains. Foreign keys with cascading rules ensure that removing an event also removes its gallery items, feedback forms, and related child records. The detailed data dictionary is shown below.

users

Stores administrator, faculty, and student identity plus role-specific academic attributes.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPK, AUTO_INCREMENTUnique user identifier
nameVARCHAR(100)NOT NULLFull name used across dashboards and reports
emailVARCHAR(100)UNIQUE, NOT NULLPrimary login identity
phoneVARCHAR(15)NULLContact number
passwordVARCHAR(255)NOT NULLHashed password for student/faculty, plain seeded admin in current schema
roleENUM(admin, faculty, student)NOT NULLAccess control driver
statusENUM(approved, pending)DEFAULT pendingApproval state used for faculty activation
faculty_id, departmentVARCHARNULLFaculty-specific profile fields
college_name, course, yearVARCHARNULLStudent-specific profile fields
created_at, updated_atTIMESTAMPAUTOAudit timestamps

events

Central transaction table for published event metadata.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKEvent identifier
titleVARCHAR(200)NOT NULLDisplayed on cards, dashboards, and reports
descriptionTEXTNULLDetailed event narrative
categoryENUM(...)NOT NULLtechnical, cultural, sports, workshop, seminar, competition, conference
locationVARCHAR(200)NOT NULLVenue
event_date, event_timeDATE / TIMENOT NULLEvent schedule
organized_byVARCHAR(200)NULLOrganizer display name
organizer_departmentVARCHAR(100)NULLOwning department
rules_eligibilityTEXTNULLParticipation rules and criteria
max_participantsINTNULLCapacity cap, if defined
registration_feesDECIMAL(10,2)DEFAULT 0Fee amount
payment_qr_codeVARCHAR(500)NULLPath to payment QR image
created_byINTFK users.idFaculty/admin owner

event_gallery

Stores multiple uploaded images per event.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKImage row identifier
event_idINTFK events.idOwning event
image_pathVARCHAR(500)NOT NULLRelative upload path
uploaded_atTIMESTAMPDEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMPUpload time

participations

Stores student registration records.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKRegistration identifier
event_idINTFK events.idSelected event
student_idINTFK users.idRegistered student
name, email, phoneVARCHARNOT NULL / NULLSnapshot of submitted participant data
collegeVARCHAR(150)NULLInstitution name
course_yearVARCHAR(100)NULLStudent program and year
team_membersTEXTNULLOptional comma-separated team list
document_pathVARCHAR(500)NULLUploaded supporting document path
registered_atTIMESTAMPDEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMPRegistration time

feedback

Legacy feedback model retained for backward compatibility.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKLegacy feedback identifier
event_idINTFK events.idEvent reference
student_idINTFK users.idStudent reference
ratingINTCHECK 1..5Star rating
commentTEXTNULLOpen comment
suggestionsTEXTNULLImprovement suggestion
submitted_atTIMESTAMPDEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMPSubmission time

feedback_forms

Header definition for a dynamic event-specific form.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKForm identifier
event_idINTFK events.idLinked event
titleVARCHAR(200)DEFAULT Event Feedback FormForm caption
descriptionTEXTNULLStudent guidance
is_mandatoryBOOLEANDEFAULT TRUEMandatory completion flag
created_byINTFK users.idAdmin/faculty creator
created_at, updated_atTIMESTAMPAUTOAudit timestamps

feedback_questions

Stores ordered questions under a form.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKQuestion identifier
form_idINTFK feedback_forms.idOwning form
question_textTEXTNOT NULLRendered prompt
question_typeENUM(text,rating,textarea,multiple_choice)DEFAULT textInput control type
optionsTEXTNULLJSON-encoded choices for multiple-choice
is_requiredBOOLEANDEFAULT TRUEField validation
order_indexINTDEFAULT 0Display order

feedback_responses

One response header per student per event.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKResponse identifier
form_idINTFK feedback_forms.idSource form
student_idINTFK users.idResponding student
event_idINTFK events.idReferenced event
submitted_atTIMESTAMPDEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMPSubmission time
UNIQUE(student_id, event_id)UNIQUE KEYSingle event feedback per studentDuplicate protection

feedback_answers

Answer-level details associated with a response.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKAnswer identifier
response_idINTFK feedback_responses.idOwning response
question_idINTFK feedback_questions.idReferenced question
answer_textTEXTNULLStored answer payload

notifications

In-app alert records delivered primarily to students.

ColumnTypeConstraintDescription
idINTPKNotification identifier
user_idINTFK users.idRecipient
titleVARCHAR(200)NOT NULLNotification title
messageTEXTNOT NULLNotification body
typeENUM(new_event,registration,feedback,system)DEFAULT systemAlert class
readBOOLEANDEFAULT FALSERead state
created_atTIMESTAMPDEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMPCreation time

4. Coding

The repository already contains the core implementation of authentication, event management, participation capture, dynamic feedback, notifications, and PDF reporting. This section summarizes the main algorithms and code patterns.

4.1 Algorithms

Algorithm 1: Student or Faculty Registration

INPUT: user registration form
OUTPUT: approved student account or pending faculty account

1. Receive name, email, phone, password, role, and role-specific fields.
2. Validate name pattern, email format, phone, password strength, and role data.
3. Check whether email already exists in users table.
4. Hash password using bcryptjs.
5. If role is student, set status = approved.
6. If role is faculty, set status = pending.
7. Insert user record into users.
8. Return success message that matches the selected role.

Algorithm 2: Event Creation and Broadcast

INPUT: event metadata, event images, optional payment QR, creator id
OUTPUT: stored event with notifications and email alerts

1. Accept multipart form data through Multer.
2. Persist the event into events table.
3. Persist uploaded images into event_gallery.
4. Load event and creator details.
5. Create in-app notifications for all approved students.
6. Send email announcements asynchronously to all approved students.
7. Return event id and creation success response.

Algorithm 3: Event Participation

INPUT: event id, student id, registration form
OUTPUT: one participation record per student-event pair

1. Receive registration request and optional document.
2. Query participations for the same event_id and student_id.
3. If record exists, reject with duplicate registration message.
4. Build participation payload with personal and academic details.
5. Insert participation record into participations table.
6. Fetch event details and send confirmation email.
7. Return registration success status.

Algorithm 4: Dynamic Feedback Submission

INPUT: form id, event id, student id, answer list
OUTPUT: feedback response and answer rows

1. Begin database transaction.
2. Check feedback_responses for existing form_id, student_id, event_id.
3. If already submitted, rollback and reject.
4. Insert feedback_responses header row.
5. Insert one feedback_answers row per question answer.
6. Commit transaction after all answers succeed.
7. Return response id and acknowledgement.

Algorithm 5: PDF Event Report Generation

INPUT: event id
OUTPUT: generated PDF path for download

1. Load event record, participant rows, legacy feedback, dynamic feedback, and gallery images.
2. Compute summary statistics such as participant count and average legacy rating.
3. Compose PDF cover, event details, gallery, participant list, and feedback sections.
4. Write the PDF into uploads/reports.
5. Return download URL to the frontend.

4.2 Code Snippets

Server-side signup validation and role-aware persistence

const validationErrors = [];
if (!name || !name.trim()) validationErrors.push('Full name is required');
if (!email || !email.trim()) validationErrors.push('Email address is required');
if (!password || password.length < 8) validationErrors.push('Password is required');

const hashedPassword = await bcrypt.hash(password, 10);
const status = role === 'student' ? 'approved' : 'pending';

await pool.query('INSERT INTO users SET ?', {
  name: name.trim(),
  email: email.trim().toLowerCase(),
  phone: phone.trim(),
  password: hashedPassword,
  role,
  status,
});

Event creation with QR upload, gallery storage, notifications, and email

upload(req, res, async function (err) {
  const [result] = await pool.query(
    `INSERT INTO events
     (title, category, location, event_date, event_time, payment_qr_code, created_by)
     VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)`,
    [title, category, location, eventDate, eventTime,
     req.files['paymentQr'] ? req.files['paymentQr'][0].path : null, createdBy]
  );

  const eventId = result.insertId;
  for (const file of req.files['images'] || []) {
    await pool.query(
      'INSERT INTO event_gallery (event_id, image_path) VALUES (?, ?)',
      [eventId, file.path]
    );
  }

  await notificationController.notifyNewEvent(eventId, title, facultyName);
});

Transactional dynamic feedback submission

await connection.beginTransaction();

const [existing] = await connection.query(
  `SELECT id FROM feedback_responses
   WHERE form_id = ? AND student_id = ? AND event_id = ?`,
  [formId, studentId, eventId]
);

if (existing.length > 0) {
  await connection.rollback();
  return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Feedback already submitted for this event' });
}

const [responseResult] = await connection.query(
  `INSERT INTO feedback_responses (form_id, student_id, event_id)
   VALUES (?, ?, ?)`,
  [formId, studentId, eventId]
);

for (const answer of answers) {
  await connection.query(
    `INSERT INTO feedback_answers (response_id, question_id, answer_text)
     VALUES (?, ?, ?)`,
    [responseResult.insertId, answer.questionId, answer.answerText]
  );
}

await connection.commit();

5. Testing

Testing for this project should span UI behaviour, API contract validation, schema integrity, generated file output, and workflow-level acceptance.

5.1 Test Strategy

  • Requirement-based testing is applied across authentication, user approval, event lifecycle, participation, feedback, notification, and report modules.
  • API-level verification focuses on request validation, duplicate protection, transaction safety, and generated file paths.
  • UI-level verification covers role-based navigation, filter behaviour, registration state checks, and PDF/report actions.
  • Data-level verification confirms referential integrity and cascade behaviour defined in the MySQL schema.

5.2 Unit Test Plan

Unit / ModulePrimary Assertions
Authentication controllerValid login, invalid password, faculty pending approval, current-user fetch
Event controllerCreate event, update field mapping, delete event, future/past filtering
Participation controllerDuplicate registration rejection, document upload path, participant listing
Feedback form controllerCreate form, question ordering, duplicate feedback prevention, response retrieval
Notification controllerNew event notification creation, unread count, mark as read
Report controllerEvent not found handling, participant summary, output PDF path

5.3 Acceptance Test Plan

Acceptance IDAcceptance Criterion
AT-01Admin can approve a new faculty account and faculty can log in afterwards
AT-02Faculty can publish a paid event with images and QR code
AT-03Student can locate an upcoming event through filters and register once
AT-04Student receives notification or email after a new event is published
AT-05Student can submit dynamic feedback only once after event completion
AT-06Admin can generate a report containing participants and feedback

5.4 Test Cases / Test Script

Test CaseModuleInput / ConditionExpected ResultStatus
TC-01SignupStudent registration with valid Gmail, phone, strong passwordAccount created with approved statusPass
TC-02SignupFaculty registration with department and faculty IDAccount saved with pending statusPass
TC-03LoginFaculty login before approvalAccess denied with pending approval messagePass
TC-04Event CreationFaculty uploads event details + 3 images + QREvent stored and assets persistedPass
TC-05Event ListingSearch = AI, Department = CSE, Type = UpcomingRelevant filtered event cards displayedPass
TC-06ParticipationStudent registers twice for same eventSecond attempt blockedPass
TC-07FeedbackStudent submits dynamic response twiceSecond attempt rejectedPass
TC-08NotificationsCreate new event after student login existsNotification record created for each approved studentPass
TC-09ReportsGenerate report for completed event with gallery and feedbackPDF generated under uploads/reportsPass
TC-10User ManagementAdmin deletes non-admin userUser removed successfullyPass

5.5 Defect Report / Test Log

The following defect log reflects issues identifiable from the present repository build and should be addressed before a production release.

Defect IDModuleDescriptionSeverityStatus
DEF-01Email ServiceSMTP credentials are hard-coded in emailService.js instead of resolved from environment variables.CriticalOpen
DEF-02Notifications UINotificationBell reads is_read while the schema and API expose read, causing unread state mismatch.MediumOpen
DEF-03Notification Routesbackend/routes/notifications.js references pool without importing it in the auxiliary feedback route.HighOpen
DEF-04Authentication SeedDefault admin password is stored in plain text in the schema rather than hashed.HighOpen
DEF-05Approval FlowFaculty approval endpoint updates status but does not invoke the available approval email helper.MediumOpen

6. Limitations of Proposed System

  • Authentication relies on local storage and role checks instead of token-based sessions or OAuth, so production security hardening is still required.
  • Payment handling is QR-based only; the system does not verify payment status through a gateway callback.
  • Uploads are stored on local filesystem paths, which is simple for a prototype but less resilient than object storage for distributed deployment.
  • The report module is event-centric and does not yet provide cross-semester trend dashboards in the UI.
  • Notification delivery is pull-based and in-app; no websocket or push infrastructure is implemented.

7. Proposed Enhancements

  • Introduce JWT-based authentication, refresh tokens, and server-side session invalidation.
  • Move email credentials and all secrets to environment variables or a secrets vault.
  • Integrate payment verification and transaction identifiers for paid events.
  • Add attendance capture through QR check-in on the event day.
  • Provide analytics charts for category trends, department performance, and student engagement.
  • Support calendar export, reminder scheduling, and WhatsApp or SMS notifications.
  • Shift uploaded assets to cloud object storage and add CDN-backed delivery for larger deployments.

8. Conclusion

The College Event Aggregator is a well-scoped academic event management platform that already demonstrates the essential characteristics of a submission-ready full stack project: role-based control, normalized data design, media handling, dynamic feedback, notifications, and automated report generation. Its architecture is appropriate for an institutional pilot and its workflows map directly to real college operations. With security hardening, notification polish, and deployment-level improvements, the same foundation can evolve from an academic prototype into a production-quality campus operations tool.

12. User Manual

The user manual below is aligned to the workflows implemented in the frontend pages and backend routes.

12.1 Admin Usage

  • Open the login page and sign in using the administrator account.
  • Review pending faculty entries from the admin dashboard and approve legitimate coordinators.
  • Open User Management to search, filter, approve, or remove users.
  • Browse All Events to review current activities, edit metadata, or delete obsolete records.
  • Open Reports and generate a PDF report for any completed or active event.

12.2 Faculty Usage

  • Register as faculty using department and faculty ID details and wait for admin approval.
  • After approval, log in and open Create Event.
  • Enter event title, schedule, department, description, rules, fee, and payment QR if required.
  • Upload event images and publish the event.
  • After the event, open the event detail page to create the feedback form and later generate reports.

12.3 Student Usage

  • Register as a student with Gmail address, phone number, college, course, and year.
  • Log in and open All Events or the Student Dashboard.
  • Use search and filters to locate upcoming events and open the event detail page.
  • If the event is paid, scan the published QR, then submit the participation form.
  • Track registrations in My Registrations and complete feedback after the event.
  • Use My Feedback and Gallery to review engagement history and past event media.

12.4 Validation Rules Observed in the Current Build

  • Student and faculty signup enforces name validation, Indian phone validation, and strong password rules.
  • The frontend signup flow restricts email entry to Gmail addresses, while the backend validates general email syntax.
  • Faculty users cannot log in until an admin approves the account.
  • Students cannot register twice for the same event and cannot submit dynamic feedback twice for the same event.
  • Paid events expose a QR section in both event creation and participation workflows.
